coredata.dc:title: A facile one step hydrothermal synthesis of carbon quantum dots for label -free fluorescence sensing approach to detect picric acid in aqueous solution
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Khan, Zubair M.S.H
Saifi, Shabeena
Shumaila,
Aslam, Zubair
Khan, Shamshad A.
Zulfequar, M.
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a facile one step approach has been applied for the synthesis of nitrogen and sulphur co-doped water soluble carbon quantum dots (NS-CQDs) through hydrothermal treatment of L-Lysine and thiourea. The obtained NS-CQDs have a high quantum yield (QY) of 53.19 % and emit strong blue fluorescence under UV light excitation of wavelength 365 nm. The morphology of NS-CQDs is spherical in shape and their sizes are distributed in the range 5–8 nm with average diameter 6.86 nm. Moreover the NS-CQDs show excitation dependent emission behavior due to quantum confinement effect. Additionally, NS-CQDs serve as a fluorescent probe for the selective and sensitive detection of picric acid (PA) in aqueous solution. A good linear response to PA in the concentration range 1–10 μM with a detection limit 0.24 μM has been obtained. The high selectivity of PA by NS-CQDs is suggested to arise from fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism. Finally these NS-CQDs fluorescent probes have been examined in real water sample by measuring the concentration of PA in tap water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Quantum yield
Fluorescence
Picric acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology and surface study of NS-CQDs
Fluorescence strategy for the detection of PA
Study of possible fluorescence quenching mechanism
Detection of PA spiked in real water samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors ZMSHK and SS are thankful to CSIR New Delhi, India for providing Junior Research Fellowship. Shumaila is grateful to University Grants Commission (UGC) for providing PDFWM. The authors are also thankful to IUAC New Delhi, India For TEM characterization facility.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of NS-CQDs (scale bar is 50 nm, Inset: the size distribution histogram). (b)TEM image of NS-CQDs at scale bar 20 nm. (c) XRD pattern of NS-CQDs (d) FTIR spectra of NS-CQDs.
(a) Full scale XPS spectrum of NS-CQDs (b) C1s XPS spectrum (c) N1s XPS spectrum (d) O1s XPS spectrum (e) S2p XPS spectrum.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of NS-CQDs; Inset shows image of NS-CQDs under normal light and under UV lamp (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of NS-CQDs at different excitation wavelength.

(a) Response of different pH values on fluorescence intensity of NS-CQDs excited at 360 nm. (b) Effect of different concentrations of KCl on fluorescence intensity of CQDs (c) fluorescence intensity of NS-CQDs at different incubation time. F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of NS-CQDs in absence and presence of different external agents.

Fluorescence quenching response of NS-CQDs in presence of different metal ions and PA (b) Fluorescence quenching response of NS-CQDs in presence of different aromatic and nitro aromatic compounds. (c) fluorescence quenching response of NS-CQDs when PA is added with other nitro aromatic analytes.

Fluorescence response of NS-CQDs when different concentrations of PA are added into the blank sample. (b) Relationship curve between the fluorescence intensity variation with the different concentrations of PA (c) Linear plot between fluorescence variation and concentration of PA.
(a) Overlap between absorption spectra of PA and emission spectra of NS-CQDs (b) fluorescence decay curve of NS-CQDs with and without PA.
Schematic diagram for synthesis of NS-CQDs and detection for picric acid.
Comparison of the proposed work with other existing literature.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: ET: Electron Transfer. EI: Electrostatic Interaction. IFE: Inner Filter Effect. PET: Photoinduced electron transfer.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recovery test for the detection of spiked PA in tap water (n = 3).

head.ce:title: A facile one step hydrothermal synthesis of carbon quantum dots for label -free fluorescence sensing approach to detect picric acid in aqueous solution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Jamia Millia Islamia, New Delhi, 110025, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Jamia Millia Islamia, New Delhi, 110025, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, St. Andrews College, Gorakhpur, U.P., 273001, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, St. Andrews College, Gorakhpur,U.P. 273001, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a facile one step approach has been applied for the synthesis of nitrogen and sulphur co-doped water soluble carbon quantum dots (NS-CQDs) through hydrothermal treatment of L-Lysine and thiourea. The obtained NS-CQDs have a high quantum yield (QY) of 53.19 % and emit strong blue fluorescence under UV light excitation of wavelength 365 nm. The morphology of NS-CQDs is spherical in shape and their sizes are distributed in the range 5–8 nm with average diameter 6.86 nm. Moreover the NS-CQDs show excitation dependent emission behavior due to quantum confinement effect. Additionally, NS-CQDs serve as a fluorescent probe for the selective and sensitive detection of picric acid (PA) in aqueous solution. A good linear response to PA in the concentration range 1–10 μM with a detection limit 0.24 μM has been obtained. The high selectivity of PA by NS-CQDs is suggested to arise from fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism. Finally these NS-CQDs fluorescent probes have been examined in real water sample by measuring the concentration of PA in tap water.

ce:section.ce:para: Picric acid also known as 2,4,6 tri nitro phenol (TNP) is a nitro aromatic explosive compound with high explosive capacity and is environmentally harmful. Picric acid (PA) has been used in landmines for decades and is currently being used in dye industries, rocket fuels, preparation of matches and fireworks [1–3]. PA is easily soluble in water and as a result it can endanger human health through groundwater and soil. It has been found that bio transformed compound of picric acid in sea water is more harmful and toxic as compared to the parent compound, which is a serious threat to surrounding environment and human. PA when inhaled or touched can cause weakness, headache, respiratory problem, eye irritation and skin damage [4–8]. Presence of strong electron withdrawing nitro group makes PA highly acidic, as a result small amount of PA can cause serious water and soil pollution. PA is poorly biodegradable and produces lasting adverse effect. “Drinking water health standards of China” (GB5749-2006) and surface water environmental quality standard (GB3838-2002) regulate that the permissible concentration of PA in water should be less than 2.18 μM [9,10]. Hence to develop a sensor for simple, rapid, selective and sensitive detection of PA in aqueous solution is highly required. Moreover due to similarity in nitro aromatic compounds it is very difficult to distinguish them, which restricts the practical application of many techniques in their detection [11]. In recent years various analytical techniques have been used for the detection of nitro aromatic explosives including surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) [12–14], X-Ray imaging technique [15], High performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [16], solid phase micro extraction [17], mass spectrometry [18], and electrochemistry [19]. Unfortunately these methods are limited due to their sophisticated operation procedure, complicated synthesis, complex labeling procedure, high cost and more time consumption. Compared to these analytical tools fluorescence based detection method have gained more attention due to its fast response, high selectivity, sensitivity, portability and easy manipulation. To date for PA detection, fluorescent chemo sensors, traditional fluorescent organic dye and metal organic framework has been explored as fluorescent probes [20–22]. But due to high toxicity, poor photo stability, complex and time consuming synthesis these fluorescent material are deficient. In contrast, fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are novel member of nano carbon family which has attracted attention of the researchers due to its unique properties such as low toxicity, good biocompatibility, stable photoluminescence, good water solubility and easy route synthesis as compared from those traditional dyes [23–26]. Till date many approaches has been carried out to synthesize CQDs such as pyrolysis [27], laser ablation [28,29], microwave irradiation [30], chemical oxidation [31] and hydrothermal/solvothermal methods [32]. However these methods, except hydrothermal, requires sophisticated instruments, strong acid treatment and have uncontrolled reaction conditions. The hydrothermal method is widely used due to its simple and efficient process with mild reaction conditions. The fluorescent properties of CQDs has been utilized as a sensor or fluorescent probe for the detection of various metal ions such as Cu2+, Hg2+, Fe3+ and for the detection of small organic molecules such as ascorbic acid, citric acid etc [33–36]. CQDs have also been used as a promising material in drug delivery and in enzyme detection. Cong Tang et.al reported detection strategy for glycosidase enzyme activity and inhibitor screening based on functional carbon dots [37]. Guilin Chen.et.al developed a unique redox-controlled fluorescent nanoswitch based on reversible disulfide bonds linked to CQDs and developed a non fluroscent turn on assay strategy for Butyrylcholinesterase (BChE) enzyme detection [38]. Efforts are being carried out to enhance the fluorescent properties and quantum yield of CQDs. Functanilaztion of CQDs surface or heteroatom doping are efficient ways to increase fluorescent properties, quantum yield (QY) and surface characteristics. Researchers have found that both heteroatom doping and surface passivation can increase the QY [39]. Heteroatom doping suppresses non radiative electron hole pair recombination and leads to high QY of CQDs [40]. Niu.et.al synthesized amine capped carbon dots using citric acid and urea with quantum yield 13% and limit of detection (LOD) of picric acid in water was 1 μM [41]. Ju et.al synthesized yellow green emission carbon dots with QY 13.2% and LOD was 2 μM [42]. Sun et.al prepared nitrogen doped carbon dots with QY 20% and can detect 0.25 μM concentration of PA in water [43]. For improvement of QY of CQDs and its sensitivity and selectivity towards PA in water, exploring effective sensor based on CQDs is highly desired.

Herein, we report the synthesis of nitrogen and sulphur doped water soluble carbon quantum dots prepared through hydrothermal approach using L-Lysine and thiourea. The procedure used for synthesis of NS-CQDs is shown in Scheme 1 . A high quantum yield of 53.19% was obtained by this procedure. The prepared NS-CQDs shows high selectivity towards picric acid in aqueous solution, therefore NS-CQDs serves as sensor towards selective and sensitive detection of PA. The limit of detection of sensor is 0.24 μM in the linear range 1–10 μm. The high selectivity towards PA is due to Fluorescence resonance energy transfer mechanism. Further the application of as- prepared NS-CQDs sensor has been confirmed in real water by measuring the concentration of PA in water sample collected from tap water of our laboratory.

Aniline, 2,4 dinitrophenol (2,4- DNP), phenol, toluene and 3 nitrophenol were purchased from Merck India. Benzoic acid (BA), Picric acid (PA), diphenyl amine and nitro benzene were supplied by Loba Chemie Pvt.Ltd India. 4 Bromo Aniline and methyl bezene (MB) was purchased from spectrochem Pvt.Ltd India. P-Anisidine, Thiourea and L-lysine was supplied by Central Drug House (P) Ltd. India. Different metal cation solution used in this report were prepared from their respective chloride salt. All the chemicals are of analytical grade and was used without any further purification. Deionized double distilled water was used throughout the whole experiment.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) micrographs were taken from JEOL (JEM-F200) electron microscope at an accelerating voltage 200 kV. The x-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern were measured from Rigaku smart lab with CuKα radiation (λ = 1.54059 Å). Fourier Transform Infra-red spectra (FT-IR) was measured from Perkin Elmer system. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out by VersaProbe III (XPS) Surface Analysis Instrument. UV–vis spectroscopy (UV–vis) data was recorded from Campsec M550 double beam scanning spectrophotometer. The fluorescence life time was determined by Time correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) setup (FL920, Edinburgh Instruments, UK). All fluorescence spectra measurement has been done using Agilent Cary Eclipse spectrophotometer.

The NS-CQDs were prepared through simple hydrothermal method using L-Lysine as carbon source and thiourea as sulphur and nitrogen source. Firstly 0.5 g of L-Lysine and 0.5 g thiourea is added in 20 mL ultrapure water and the mixture was ultrasonicated for 20 min. After that the solution was transferred to a 60 mL teflon lined hydrothermal autoclave and was kept in furnace for 5 h at 220 °C. After cooling it naturally at room temperature, a light yellow solution was obtained. This yellow solution was centrifuged for ten minutes at 10,000 rpm and supernatant was collected. Further this supernatant was filtered through 0.22 μm syringe filter to get pure CQDs.

Quantum yield (QY) of CQDs was calculated by taking quinine sulfate as a reference. CQDs was dissolved in ultrapure water whereas quinine sulfate with QY 0.54 was dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4. To reduce the re- absorption effect the absorbance of CQDs were kept below 0.1. The QY of CQDs was determined by using the Eq.1 [44]
(1)
∅ c
=
∅ R
×
I c
I R
×
A R
A c
×
η c 2
η R 2
Where ∅ is QY of the sample (CQD). I is integrated emission intensity, A is absorbance measured at 360 nm and η is refractive index. The subscript c is assigned for sample (CQD) whereas R refers to reference (Quinine sulfate).

At room temperature, 100 μL (0.2 mg/ml) of NS-CQDs were mixed with 400 μL of 0.2 M sodium acetate buffer at pH 6. Subsequently 100 μL of different concentrations of PA was added and diluted to 2.5 ml by adding distilled water. The solution was then transferred to quartz cuvette and after 3 min of reaction time, fluorescence spectra were taken at excitation wavelength of 360 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and surface study of NS-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the size and morphology of as prepared NS-CQDs, TEM measurement was performed. Fig. 1 (a,b) shows TEM micrograph of NS-CQDs at two different scale bars. It can be seen from the TEM micrograph that the particles are spherical in shape and are well separated from each other. The size of the dots is distributed in the range 5–8 nm with an average diameter 6.86 nm. XRD pattern shown in Fig. 1(c) shows broad hump around 2θ = 23.60 which corresponds to (002) plane of CQDs and suggests the amorphous and graphitic nature of the prepared carbon material. This amorphous nature arises due to doping of nitrogen and sulphur into the core of carbon [45].

FTIR spectra were taken to explore the surface groups present on the surface of the CQDs. As shown in Fig. 1(d) the broad absorption band centered at 3281 cm−1 are assigned to stretching vibration of NH and OH [46]. Strong peak present at 1634 cm−1 is assigned to amide linkage −CONH- and stretching vibration of CO [47], which demonstrate the attachment of amide group on the surface of CQDs. The Peak at 1461 cm−1 is CH3 bending vibrations whereas peak at 1408 cm−1 is C–N stretching vibration. The results of FTIR clearly show existence of O rich groups which imparts solubility and dispensability of CQDs in aqueous medium.

Further, XPS measurements were carried out to explore the chemical composition and chemical bonding between the elements. Fig. 2 (a) shows the wide full scan XPS spectrum with four distinct peaks at 283.5 eV, 399.4 eV, 530.2 eV and 162.4 eV which corresponds to C1s, N1s, O1s and S2p respectively. The high resolution C1s spectrum in Fig. 2(b) shows three peaks with binding energies around 283.8 eV,284.8 eV, and 287.2 eV which are assigned to CC/C-C, C–N, CO respectively. The N1s spectrum in Fig. 2 (c) is de-convoluted into two peaks with binding energies at 398.8 eV and 400.6 eV which is attributed to C–N-C and NH bonds. Fig. 2(d) shows O1s spectrum of bonds CO (530.8 eV) and COH/C-O-C (532.56 eV). Moreover, the S2p spectrum shows peaks at 162.2 eV and 163.8 eV which corresponds to S2p3/2 and S2p1/2 of thiopene S owing to their spin orbit coupling. The results of XPS show existence of amide groups and also confirm the doping of nitrogen and sulphur atoms in the prepared carbon quantum dots.

UV-Vis spectroscopy was carried out to investigate the optical properties of the prepared NS- CQDs in aqueous solution. As shown in Fig. 3 (a) there is a presence of strong absorption peak at 267 nm which is attributed to π-π* transition of CC [48]. The inset photograph in Fig. 3(a) shows that NS-CQDs solution is faint yellow in day light whereas under UV lamp of wavelength 365 nm these CQDs shows bright blue fluorescence. Fig. 3(b) shows fluorescence emission spectra of NS- CQDs at different excitation wavelength ranging from 280 nm to 400 nm. The emission spectra show excitation dependent behavior which is very common in CQDs. The maximum fluorescence intensity is observed at wavelength 300 nm. On increasing the excitation wavelength the emission intensity decreases and emission slowly shifts towards longer wavelength. The excitation dependent fluorescence of CQDs may have arised due to various reasons. Firstly, the FTIR spectra confirm the presence of functional groups like −COOH and −OH on the surface of CQDs indicating inhomogeneous chemical structure and abundant trap states. The fluorescence from CQDs is basically from these emissive traps and functional groups while the excitation dependent emission is due to the fact that at a particular wavelength certain emissive sites can be excited [49]. The increase in excitation energy correspondingly increases the number of sites as higher energy traps can now be excited. Secondly, the discreteness of phonon density of states in nanostructures is also responsible for excitation dependent behavior of CQDs [50]. However the most widely adopted mechanism for explanation of excitation dependent behavior is quantum confinement or size effect [51]. The energy bandgap of quantum dots are determined by the size of the dots and it is inversely proportional to the size of the dot. A smaller size leads to high energy band gap and vice- versa. Non uniform distribution of CQDs caused deviations in energy gaps which lead to broadened emission wavelength covering almost the visible region.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence strategy for the detection of PA

ce:section.ce:para: The as prepared NS- CQDs were tested in different situation in order to confirm their stability for smooth detection of PA in water samples.. The fluorescence spectra of NS- CQDs were recorded with different pH values ranging from 2 to 14 and in different ionic strength ranging from concentration 0 to 2 M. Fig. 4 (a) shows relative intensity of NS-CQDs with different pH values. Here F is fluorescence intensity after addition of different pH and F0 is intensity of the blank sample. It is clearly observed from the graph that addition of different pH values have negligible effect on fluorescence intensity of CQDs. The fluorescence is stable in the pH range 4-12 while certain degree of changes occurs in strong acidic and alkaline condition. Since pH value has little influence so detection process can be carried out in broad pH range. Finally pH 6 was chosen throughout the experiment for best results. Further for investigating ionic strength on fluorescence intensity of CQDs, different concentration of KCl is added. Fig. 4(b)shows that even on adding high concentration of KCl (2 M) the intensity does not significantly changes. It concludes that NS-CQDs are stable in high ionic environment conditions. To get the optimal incubation time, fluorescence intensity on NS- CQDs in the presence of PA was investigated. When PA was added to NS- CQDs, the fluorescence intensity of the mixture decreases sharply with in 30 s. and stabilizes within 3 min as shown in Fig. 4(c) So optimal incubation time 3 min was selected for PA determination.

Selectivity is an important parameter for an efficient sensor. Selectivity was tested by monitoring the change in fluorescence intensity of NS- CQDs in the presence of various nitro aromatics, aromatic (Aniline, Benzoic acid, Di phenyl amine, 4 Bromo Aniline, 2–4 DNP, P-Anisidine, phenol, MB, TNT, toluene, 3 Nitro phenol, nitro benzene) and different metal ions (Ni2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Na+, Mg2+, K+). Fig. 5 (a) shows that the fluorescence intensity of NS- CQDs decreases when PA (500 μM) is added into it. Whereas other metal ions (500 μM) when added into NS- CQDs solution does not leads to significant variation of fluorescence intensity which implies that present sensor is highly specific for selecting PA in different metal ions. Further Fig. 5(b) shows that on addition of same concentration (500 μM) of different aromatic and nitro aromatic in NS-CQDs solution, the fluorescence intensity of only PA is effectively quenched whereas the effects of other analytes on fluorescence intensity is negligible. The ratio of F/F0 vs different analytes were calculated to illustrate the comparison. Here F0 is emission from blank sample and F is emission after adding different analytes. The ratio of other analytes were atleast 8 times higher than that of PA. The interference of highly interfering analytes 2–4 DNP, BA and diphenyl amine was also studied. Fig. 5(c) show that even on addition of other analytes with PA there was no further decrease in fluorescence intensity.

To investigate the fluorescence intensity of NS-CQDs with increasing concentration of PA, different concentrations of PA (0–500 μM) were added in the CQD solution at optimum condition. Fig. 6 (a) shows that on increasing the concentration of PA fluorescence intensity decreases with slight shift in emission towards longer wavelength at high concentration of PA. This shifting towards longer wavelength reveals the electrostatic interactions and hydroxyl groups present in PA. Fig. 6(b) shows the graph between relative fluorescence intensity vs. different concentration of PA. The fluorescence quenching efficiency is linear in the concentration range 1–10 μM Fig. 6(c) with correlation factor (R2) = 0.99 and can be best described by the given linear Eq.2
(2) F0/F -1 = 0.0108 (PA) + .0531
The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated from the relation

LOD = 3 σ/K [10] where σ is standard deviation and K is the slope of the regression line. The LOD calculated comes out to be 0.24 μM which is comparable and better than many reported results summarized in Table 1

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of possible fluorescence quenching mechanism
ce:section.ce:para:
The possible mechanism for the selective response of CQDs to PA has been investigated. Fluorescence quenching is either due to Forester resonance energy transfer (FRET) or inner filter effect (IFE). FRET is considered to be dominant quenching mechanism at surface of CQDs [52]. The concept behind FRET is due to the fact that it involves a donor flurophore which in excited state transfer its excitation energy to a acceptor chromophore in a non radiative way through long range dipole dipole interactions [53]. FRET is an electrodynamics phenomenon and can be well explained by classical physics. The excited donor flurophore acts as a oscillating dipole that can exchange energy with second dipole having same resonance frequency. FRET occurs without emitting photon due to large range dipole dipole interaction between donor flurophore and acceptor chromophore. Interaction between pair of molecules that results into FRET to occur are called donor acceptor pair. FRET does not require acceptor chromophore to be fluorescent, although in many applications both the donor acceptor pair are fluorescent. In our study CQDs are donor flurophore and is fluorescent whereas picric acid (PA) is acceptor chromophore and it is non fluorescent. There are certain criteria that should be satisfied in order to demonstrate that the quenching mechanism is due to FRET. These criteria are (a) The fluorescence emission spectra of donor flurophore (CQDs) should overlap with the absorption spectra of acceptor chromophore (Picric acid) [54,55]. (b) The fluorescence life time of CQDs should decrease on addition of PA (quenching agent) [56]. (c) CQDs –PA must be close to each other (typically) 10 Å–100 Å) [57,58]. Fig. 7 (a) shows a significant overlap between emission spectra of CQDs and absorption spectra of quenching agent (PA), which suggest either FRET or IFE (Inner filter effect) mechanism behind fluorescence quenching of CQDs. For further confirming the quenching mechanism TCSPC measurement has been carried out. TCSPC was used to measure life time of CQDs in absence and presence of PA. Fig. 7 (b) shows the life time spectra of CQDs with and without PA, The obtained results were best fitted with bi exponential fit and the emission intensity decay I (t) is given by the Eq.3
(3)
I
t
=
B
1
exp
-
t
τ 1
+
B
2
e x p
-
t
τ 2
Where τ1, and τ2 are the time constants of the two decay channels and B1, B2 are the their corresponding amplitudes. Avergae life time <τ> were obtained from the Eq.4
(4)
< τ >
=
B 1
τ
1
2
+
B 2
τ 2 2
B 1
τ 1
+
B 2
τ 2

And the average life time was calculated out to be 4.78 ns without PA and 4.58 ns with PA. The results suggest that on presence of PA there is a decrease in fluorescence life time. Since due to decrease in life time. IFE mechanism is ruled out [39]. The two main criteria for FRET mechanism that there should be significant overlap between emission of CQDs and absorption of PA, and decrease in life time with PA has been satisfied. So quenching of CQDs here in our reported work is truly due to energy transfer mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of PA spiked in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The as prepared NS-CQDs fluorescent probe sensor was further tested in real water samples to validate its applicability. Water samples were collected from the tap water of our laboratory and filtered using 0.22 μm syringe filter. Since no PA was found in the tap water, therefore a recovery procedure was carried out by spiking different concentrations of PA in the water samples. Table 2 shows that the spiked and obtained values are in good agreement with recovery in the range 95.7–105%. The relative standard deviation of three replicates of each sample is less than 10% which suggests its high reproducibility and feasibility.

A facile one step hydrothermal method is applied to synthesize nitrogen, sulphur co-doped carbon quantum dots (NS-CQDs) using L-Lysine and thiourea. The obtained NS-CQDs under UV lamp shows blue fluorescence and has a high quantum yield of 53.19%.The NS-CQDs are water soluble, pH independent and are stable at high ionic concentrations. The high selectivity of NS-CQDs to PA due to FRET mechanism has been utilized to construct a fluorescent probe for sensitive, selective and fast detection of PA in aqueous solution. A good linear dependence between different concentrations of PA and relative fluorescence intensity of NS-CQDs was established in the range 1–10 μM with lowest limit detection of 0.24 μM. Further these fluorescent probes based on NS-CQDs were tested in real water samples in order to check its feasibility and reproducibility. The proposed sensor is simple, inexpensive and detects rapidly, which exhibits its potential application in sensing PA in water samples.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that there is no conflict of interest in the manuscript

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors ZMSHK and SS are thankful to CSIR New Delhi, India for providing Junior Research Fellowship. Shumaila is grateful to University Grants Commission (UGC) for providing PDFWM. The authors are also thankful to IUAC New Delhi, India For TEM characterization facility.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A colorimetric and fluorescent chemosensor for the detection of an explosive—2, 4, 6-trinitrophenol (TNP)

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Complex‐formation‐enhanced fluorescence quenching effect for efficient detection of picric acid

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: -Ding, Aixiang, Longmei Yang, Yuyang Zhang, Gaobin Zhang, Lin Kong, Xuanjun Zhang, Yupeng Tian, Xutang Tao, and Jiaxiang Yang. "Complex‐Formation‐Enhanced Fluorescence Quenching Effect for Efficient Detection of Picric Acid." Chemistry–A European Journal 20, no. 38 (2014): 12215-12222.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A fluorescent organic cage for picric acid detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: -Acharyya, Koushik, and Partha Sarathi Mukherjee. "A fluorescent organic cage for picric acid detection." Chemical Communications 50, no. 99 (2014): 15788-15791.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fate and effects of picric acid and 2, 6-DNT in marine environments: toxicity of degradation products

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: -Nipper, Marion, R. Scott Carr, James M. Biedenbach, Russell L. Hooten, and Karen Miller. "Fate and effects of picric acid and 2, 6-DNT in marine environments: Toxicity of degradation products." Marine pollution bulletin 50, no. 11 (2005): 1205-1217.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One step hydrothermal synthesis of carbon nanodots to realize the fluorescence detection of picric acid in real samples

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: -Li, Junfen, Lin Zhang, Pengxia Li, Yan Zhang, and Chuan Dong. "One step hydrothermal synthesis of carbon nanodots to realize the fluorescence detection of picric acid in real samples." Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical 258 (2018): 580-588.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Conducting carbon dot–polypyrrole nanocomposite for sensitive detection of picric acid

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: -Pal, Ayan, Md Palashuddin Sk, and Arun Chattopadhyay. "Conducting carbon dot–polypyrrole nanocomposite for sensitive detection of picric acid." ACS applied materials & interfaces 8, no. 9 (2016): 5758-5762.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel picric acid film sensor via combination of the surface enrichment effect of chitosan films and the aggregation-induced emission effect of siloles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: -He, Gang, Haonan Peng, Taihong Liu, Meini Yang, Yuan Zhang, and Yu Fang. Journal of Materials Chemistry 19, no. 39 (2009): 7347-7353.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent tris-imidazolium sensors for picric acid explosive

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrothermal synthesis of carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes for the sensitive and rapid detection of picric acid
